turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion on the Court this morning in case 15-1248, the McLane Company versus the EEOC which I will read for her. Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits employment discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. It entrust the enforcement of that probation to the Equal Opportune Employment Opportunity Commission or EEOC and authorizes the EEOC to issue subpoenas in order to obtain evidence relevant to its investigations. If an employer refuses to comply with such a subpoena, the EEOC may file suit in federal court and ask the court for an order enforcing the subpoena. This case is about the standard of review that a court of appeals should apply when reviewing a District Court's decision whether to enforce an EEOC subpoena. Petitioner McLane Company is a supply chain services company. In 2008, at the request of one of McLane's employees, the EEOC began an investigation into whether McLane's use of a physical evaluation as a tool to assess its employee's ability to perform their job duties discriminated on the basis of their age and gender. The EEOC sought certain evidence from McLane via a subpoena which McLane refused to provide. The agency filed suit to enforce the subpoena, but the district court declined to enforce it. The Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit applying circuit precedent reviewed the district court's decision de novo and reversed, but it questioned whether its own precedent requiring de novo review was correct observing that other circuits applied differential review to a district court's decision whether to enforce an EEOC subpoena. We hold that a district court's decision whether to enforce such a subpoena should be reviewed for abuse of discretion. We reach that conclusion for two primary reasons. First the history of appellate practice points in that direction. For decades, every Court of Appeals except the Ninth Circuit has reviewed decisions like this for abuse of discretion. Second, basic considerations of institutional capacity help explain why this is so. In most cases, the district court's enforcement decision will turn either on whether the evidence sought by the EEOC is relevant to the charge or whether the subpoena is unduly burdensome in light of the circumstances. These fact and circumstance sensitive tasks are well-suited to the expertise of a district court. Indeed they closely resemble decisions made every day by district judges in other contexts. For those reasons and for other reasons set out in the opinion, we conclude that the Court of Appeals should review a district court's decision whether to enforce an EEOC subpoena differentially not de novo. The judgment of the Ninth Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part.
